items.summary: The cokernel Z/2 of 033's φ is identified concretely with P^∨(B_2) / Q^∨(B_2). The index-2 obstruction is therefore the SAME Z/2 as the centre of the simply-connected B_2-group (Spin(5) = Sp(2)). This was implicit in 033 but was not framed in P^∨/Q^∨ language there; the explicit identification clarifies why the obstruction is rigid — it is a topological invariant of the root system, not an artefact of the construction.
items.implication: Strengthens 033's classification: 'index-2' is the unambiguous structural answer, with a 1-line lattice-theory explanation. Suggests the M6 row could be re-phrased as 'φ realises W^aff(B_2) as the affine subgroup of W^ext(B_2) ≅ Aut(D_6^(1)) ⋉ W((2A_1)^(1)); the cokernel Z/2 is exactly P^∨(B_2)/Q^∨(B_2)'. This is a *more* elegant phrasing than 033's free-form INDEX-2 statement.

items.summary: Okamoto's §1.3 (ii) defines s : θ_0 ↦ -θ_0 - 2 (NOT -1 - θ_0). The shift '-2' is the MAXIMAL even-integer shift; the '-1' shift would lie outside W^aff(B_2). This is consistent with Okamoto's policy throughout his Painlevé-symmetry papers (1987 P_III', 1987 P_IV, 1987 P_VI) of working in the non-extended affine Weyl group and leaving the P^∨/Q^∨ extension implicit. Sakai 2001 surface theory automatically supplies the extension via Aut(D_6^(1)).
items.implication: Provides a clean historical / methodological framing for why the post-Sakai literature treats the W cross-walk as 'almost-isomorphism' rather than strict iso: Okamoto's W^aff(B_2) is genuinely a proper subgroup, and the Cremona group is genuinely the EXTENDED affine Weyl group. This is not a defect — it is a feature that needs to be named in M6 / CT v1.4 §3.5.
